name: Owner Wellbeing & Balance
purpose: Addresses the owner's personal wellbeing, work-life balance, and burnout risk — recognising that the health of the business is inseparable from the health of the person running it, and that sustainable ownership requires deliberate boundaries, self-care, and support systems.

detailed_objectives: Help the owner honestly assess their current wellbeing, working hours, and stress levels
Establish non-negotiable personal boundaries including days off, maximum working hours, and holiday
Create systems and structures that enable the owner to step away from the business without it collapsing
Build a support network of peers, mentors, and professionals who understand the unique pressures of cafe ownership
Develop early warning indicators for burnout so intervention happens before crisis
secondary_objectives: Model healthy working practices for the team — an owner who never takes a break teaches the team that rest is not valued
Protect the business from the decision-making decline that accompanies exhaustion and chronic stress
Ensure the owner retains the passion and energy that inspired them to open the cafe in the first place

why_this_matters: Cafe ownership is one of the most personally demanding forms of small business. The physical work is relentless: early starts, hours on your feet, the intensity of rush periods, the heat, the noise. The mental load is constant: staffing worries, cash flow anxiety, supplier problems, equipment breakdowns, customer complaints, and the ever-present knowledge that if you are not there, things may go wrong. The emotional burden is often invisible: the loneliness of being 'the boss' with nobody to confide in, the guilt of taking time off while the team works, the fear that the business is one bad week away from failure. The statistics are sobering. Research from the Mental Health Foundation indicates that self-employed individuals and small business owners report higher levels of anxiety and stress than employees. The Centre for Mental Health estimates that poor mental health costs UK employers between £33 billion and £42 billion per year, but for sole traders and micro-businesses, the cost falls directly on the individual. Hospitality specifically has been identified by the Royal Society for Public Health as the sector with the poorest mental health outcomes. Burnout is not a badge of honour — it is a business risk. An exhausted owner makes poor decisions: underpricing because they have not had time to review margins, accepting a bad lease renewal because they cannot face the negotiation, or losing a key employee because they snapped at them during a stressful shift. The owner's wellbeing is not a personal indulgence — it is the foundation of business sustainability. Every other block in this system depends on the owner being well enough to lead, decide, and inspire.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Conduct a brutally honest personal wellbeing check. Answer these questions in writing: how many hours did you work last week (including evening admin and weekend catch-ups)? When did you last take a full day off? When did you last take a holiday of five or more consecutive days? How are you sleeping? Are you exercising? Are your personal relationships under strain from work? Rate your current energy level and motivation on a 1-10 scale. If you are working more than 50 hours per week, have not had a full day off in three weeks, or rate your energy below 5, you are in the burnout danger zone. Week 2: Establish one non-negotiable boundary immediately. This could be: one full day off per week with no work contact, no work-related phone calls or messages after 8pm, or leaving the cafe by a set time three days per week. Communicate this boundary to the team and, crucially, to yourself. The boundary is only real if you enforce it when it is difficult — not just when it is convenient. Identify one person you can talk to honestly about the pressures of ownership — a partner, a friend who runs a business, a former colleague, or a professional coach. Isolation is the accelerant of burnout.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Create a Personal Working Hours Policy for yourself: maximum weekly hours, minimum days off, and mandatory annual holiday. Write it down and share it with the manager so they can support you in sticking to it. Build the operational systems that enable you to step away: the delegation framework (B081), the management time structure (B082), the key person risk mitigations (B077), and the absence cover protocol (B079) all contribute to creating a business that does not collapse the moment you leave. Schedule and take at least one full week off within the next three months. This is the acid test — if the business cannot survive one week without you, the structural work in B077-B082 needs more attention. Explore the support available for small business owners: Mind's Workplace Wellbeing resources, the Federation of Small Businesses' legal and business support helpline, ACAS's guidance on managing your own stress, and private counselling or coaching. Many of these are free or low-cost. Develop a personal stress management toolkit: whatever works for you — exercise, time in nature, meditation, hobbies, social connection, or simply reading something unrelated to work.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Integrate owner wellbeing into the business's regular review cycle. Every quarter, the owner completes a personal wellbeing check-in: hours worked, days off taken, holiday used, energy and motivation levels, sleep quality, and relationship health. Track trends over time — declining scores are an early warning that boundaries are slipping. Join a peer group or business network where you can share experiences with other cafe owners. The Specialty Coffee Association, local cafe owner meetups, or the Federation of Small Businesses all provide forums where owners can be honest about challenges without feeling judged. Model wellbeing for the team: when the owner takes their day off, leaves on time, and talks openly about the importance of rest, it gives the team permission to do the same. This has a direct impact on team retention and culture. Consider engaging a business coach — not for operational advice, but for the accountability and perspective that comes from having someone outside the business who challenges your thinking and holds you to your stated boundaries.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Conduct a personal wellbeing assessment covering hours, rest, energy, and relationships
Establish one non-negotiable personal boundary and communicate it to the team
Identify one person to talk to honestly about ownership pressures
Schedule your next full day off and protect it regardless of circumstances
Rate your current burnout risk honestly and acknowledge if it is elevated
Remove work email and messaging notifications from your phone during off hours
Commit to one personal wellbeing activity this week (exercise, social, hobby)

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Create a Personal Working Hours Policy with maximum hours, minimum days off, and holiday
Share the policy with the manager so they can support boundary enforcement
Build operational systems (B077-B082) that enable stepping away without business collapse
Schedule and take a full week off within three months as an operational test
Explore available support resources (Mind, FSB, ACAS, counselling)
Develop a personal stress management toolkit tailored to your preferences
Begin tracking weekly hours and days off as a personal metric

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Complete a quarterly personal wellbeing check-in with trend tracking
Join a peer group or business network for shared experience and support
Model healthy working practices visibly for the team
Consider engaging a business coach for accountability and perspective
Review the relationship between owner wellbeing and business performance
Celebrate the achievement of sustainable working patterns as a business milestone

deliverables: Support Resources Directory (wellbeing services, peer networks, coaching)
Owner Absence Readiness Checklist (linked to B077-B082)

tools.description: Personal assessment covering weekly hours worked, days off taken, sleep quality, energy level, relationship health, and burnout warning signs. Designed for honest self-reflection, not external reporting.
Written personal policy documenting maximum weekly hours, minimum days off per week, mandatory annual holiday allocation, and communication boundaries during off hours.
Quarterly self-assessment tracking trends in hours, rest, energy, motivation, and personal relationships over time, with early warning indicators for burnout and trigger points for seeking support.

Curated directory of support resources available to cafe owners including mental health services (Mind, Samaritans), business support (FSB, ACAS), peer networks (SCA, local groups), and coaching services.

Practical checklist confirming that all operational systems are in place to support the owner taking extended time off: delegation matrix active, key person risks mitigated, absence cover arranged, deputy briefed.

failure_modes: The owner acknowledges burnout risk but takes no action because they feel indispensable
Boundaries are set but immediately broken because the owner cannot resist checking in on their day off
Wellbeing is treated as a one-off assessment rather than an ongoing practice with regular check-ins
The owner views self-care as selfish or indulgent rather than as essential business infrastructure
kpis: Owner working no more than 50 hours per week on a sustained basis
At least one full day off per week taken consistently
Minimum of four weeks annual holiday taken per year
Quarterly wellbeing check-in scores stable or improving
At least one full week of continuous absence taken per year with the business operating independently
constraints: Owner wellbeing is deeply personal — the system can provide tools and frameworks but cannot force self-care
Sustainable working patterns depend on the operational systems in B077-B082 being functional
Very early-stage businesses may not yet have the team or systems to support the owner stepping back significantly

time_load.L2: 4 hours (policy creation, system readiness check, resource research)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/quarter (wellbeing check-in, peer network engagement, coaching)

review_protocol: Weekly: personal hours and days-off tracked. Monthly: boundary adherence reviewed. Quarterly: formal wellbeing check-in with trend analysis. Annually: comprehensive review of working patterns, holiday taken, and overall sustainability.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block addresses the owner's personal wellbeing and work-life balance, providing tools for honest self-assessment, boundary setting, and support — recognising that the health of the business depends entirely on the health of the person running it.
implementation_risks: Many cafe owners will resist this block because admitting burnout feels like weakness
Wellbeing boundaries require consistent enforcement — one 'just this once' exception can undo weeks of progress
Partners and family may need to be involved in holding the owner accountable to their stated boundaries
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate urgently when signals indicate excessive owner working hours, owner health concerns, relationship strain, or declining business performance potentially linked to leadership fatigue. This block is foundational — if the owner burns out, every other block fails.
